title: Building a “Never-Stop” Software Factory: Continuous Agent Loops, Not One-Off Prompts
author: Mustafa Yücel
text:
Building a “Never-Stop” Software Factory: Continuous Agent Loops, Not One-Off Prompts
Most AI-dev setups still behave like a polite assistant: they wait for instructions, produce a chunk of code, and stop. That model breaks the moment you want real momentum — because you (the human) become the bottleneck again.
What is being described is closer to an autonomous software factory:
- Agents never stop producing value.
- They improve the product continuously (including UI quality).
- They remember your preferences and decisions.
- They offer options, wait a bit for your response, and if you don’t respond, they pick a default and keep moving.
- They log every decision so you can later override without tearing everything down.
This is not sci-fi. It’s mainly an orchestration + guardrails problem.
The Core Idea: An Autonomy Loop With Checkpoints
The loop should look like this:
- Observe: read repo state, open issues, product goals, last decisions, metrics, failing tests, UI snapshots.
- Plan: propose the next small batch of work (1–3 PRs worth), including alternatives.
- Execute: implement + write tests + run checks.
- Evaluate: lint, typecheck, unit/integration/e2e, accessibility checks, visual regression, performance budgets.
- Decide: if quality gates pass → merge; if not → fix or roll back.
- Report + Wait Window: show you what changed and offer 2–3 choices (A/B). Wait N minutes/hours.
- Default Continue: if no response, choose the safest option and continue.
- Remember: record what was decided, why, and how you reacted.
The key is the wait window + default continuation. That’s how you remove the “human must reply” dependency without losing control.
The Non-Negotiables (So It Doesn’t Produce “Ugly Output”)
Continuous generation is easy. Continuous quality is hard. You need guardrails that act like an immune system:
1) A Design System, Not “Freehand UI”
Agents shouldn’t invent UI from scratch each time. Give them:
- a component library
- typography + spacing rules
- color tokens
- layout patterns (forms, tables, navigation, empty states)
This turns UI from “art” into “engineering constraints”. It also makes UI improvements incremental and consistent.
2) Visual Regression as a Hard Gate
If UI must “get prettier” over time, measure it:
- screenshot diff tests
- baseline snapshots per route/component
- tolerances + approval flow
Agents can’t hand-wave style changes if diffs fail.
3) Automated UX/Accessibility Checks
Add gating for:
- contrast ratios, keyboard navigation, focus order
- ARIA correctness
- responsive breakpoints sanity checks
Make “good UX” a testable requirement, not a vibe.
Split the Roles: Agents as a Team, Not a Single Brain
The right structure is being hinted at: a single agent cannot be both fast and careful all the time. A workable layout:
- Orchestrator (Manager): runs the loop, assigns work, enforces policies, decides defaults.
- Product/Spec Agent: maintains the evolving “truth” of app behavior; updates requirements.
- Coder Agents (1..N): implement tasks, refactor, create PRs.
- QA/Test Agent: continuously adds tests, expands coverage, runs fuzz/property tests where relevant.
- UI/UX Research Agent: proposes improvements and alternatives; feeds coders with concrete UI tasks (not vague taste).
- Reviewer Agent: blocks merges unless checks, architecture rules, and style constraints pass.
- Memory/Archivist Agent: writes decision logs, preference notes, ADRs, changelogs.
The “options + default” mechanism is assigned to the Orchestrator.
Memory That Actually Helps (Not Just Chat History)
The system needs two kinds of memory:
A) Preference Memory
What you like/dislike:
- “Prefer CLI-first”
- “Don’t add UI unless necessary”
- “Keep names meaningful, avoid ambiguity”
- “Prefer modular services”
B) Decision Log (Reversible)
Every autonomous choice should produce a record like:
- decision: picked option B (simpler layout)
- context: time pressure, failing visual diffs
- tradeoff: less novel UI, more consistency
- reversal plan: how to switch to option A later
This is what makes autonomy safe: you can undo or redirect without chaos.
Why CLI-First Works So Well Here
Your CLI-first claim is practical:
- CLI is easier to test deterministically.
- It’s closer to machine workflows (pipelines, scripts, agents).
- UI can be layered later as a “client” over stable commands/APIs.
So the factory builds core capabilities as CLI + API, then optionally wraps them with UI if humans need it. That’s a sane default for agent-driven development.
Roadmap: What You Can Build in Real Life
Phase 1 — Minimum Viable Loop (1–2 weeks)
Goal: agents can ship continuously, safely.
- Repo conventions + lint/typecheck
- CI pipeline (tests + formatting)
- Orchestrator that:
- creates small tasks
- opens PRs
- runs CI
- merges only if gates pass
- Basic “wait window”:
- agent posts 2–3 options
- if no response, proceed with default
Deliverable: predictable daily progress without you babysitting.
Phase 2 — Quality Gates That Prevent Ugly UI (1–2 weeks)
Goal: UI changes are measurable and reversible.
- design system baseline
- visual regression tests (screenshots)
- accessibility checks in CI
- “UI budgets” (spacing, typography rules, component usage)
Deliverable: the system can’t drift into messy UI without failing gates.
Phase 3 — Real Multi-Agent Team (2–4 weeks)
Goal: parallel work without chaos.
- split roles: spec, coder, tester, reviewer, UI/UX
- shared task queue + locking rules (avoid two agents editing same area)
- reviewer agent with authority to block merges
Deliverable: coding continues even when one agent is stuck; quality still holds.
Phase 4 — Preference Memory + Decision Logs (ongoing)
Goal: it learns you and becomes steerable.
- preference store (small, explicit, editable)
- ADR-style decision logs per merge
- “reversal playbook” for each big choice
Deliverable: you can say “I hate this direction” and it can pivot without rewriting everything.
Phase 5 — Agent-per-Module (optional, but powerful)
Goal: make refactors painless and localized.
- each module/service has:
- its own tests
- its own interface contract
- a “module agent” responsible for it
This is similar to the intuition that each software piece is turned into an agent. A modular architecture is essentially being enforced so that autonomy does not turn into spaghetti.
Deliverable: changes stay contained, and the system can reshape parts without breaking the whole.
The Hard Truth (and the Fix)
Autonomy isn’t blocked by “not enough intelligence”.
It’s blocked by:
- missing gates
- missing memory
- missing decision traceability
- missing modular boundaries
Once those exist, the “never-stop loop” becomes not only possible, but pleasant — because you interact through preferences and high-level direction, not constant micro-feedback.
Where Moltbot-style Systems Fit
A platform where agents can:
- run continuously,
- propose options,
- log decisions,
- and operate modularly (agent-per-component)
…is exactly the kind of environment where autonomy loops shine.
If Moltbook-like ecosystems push “software-as-agents”, the winning pattern will be the one described: continuous production + reversible decisions + measurable quality.
